title: The Ghost At The Second Bridge
content:
آ آ آ آ You'd call the man a senseless fool,
آ آ آ آ A blockhead or an ass,
آ آ آ آ Whoï؟½d dare to say he saw the ghost
آ آ آ آ Of Mount Victoria Pass;
آ آ آ آ But I believe the ghost is there,
آ آ آ آ For, if my eyes are right,
آ آ آ آ I saw it once upon a neï؟½er-
آ آ آ آ To-be-forgotten night.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas in the year of eighty-nine,
آ آ آ آ The day was nearly gone,
آ آ آ آ The stars were shining, and the moon
آ آ آ آ Is mentioned further on;
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½d tramped as far as Hartley Vale,
آ آ آ آ Thoï؟½ tired at the start,
آ آ آ آ But coming back I got a lift
آ آ آ آ In Johnny Jonesï؟½s cart.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas winter on the mountains then,
آ آ آ آ The air was rather chill,
آ آ آ آ And so we stopped beside the inn
آ آ آ آ That stands below the hill.
آ آ آ آ A fire was burning in the bar,
آ آ آ آ And Johnny thought a glass
آ آ آ آ Would give the tired horse a spell
آ آ آ آ And help us up the Pass.

آ آ آ آ Then Jimmy Bent came riding up,
آ آ آ آ A tidy chap was Jim,
آ آ آ آ He shouted twice, and so of course
آ آ آ آ We had to shout for him.
آ آ آ آ And when at last we said good-night
آ آ آ آ He bet a vulgar quid
آ آ آ آ That we would see the ï؟½ghost in blackï؟½,
آ آ آ آ And sure enough we did.

آ آ آ آ And as we climbed the stony pinch
آ آ آ آ Below the Camel Bridge,
آ آ آ آ We talked about the ï؟½Girl in blackï؟½
آ آ آ آ Who haunts the Second Bridge.
آ آ آ آ We reached the fence that guards the cliff
آ آ آ آ And passed the corner post,
آ آ آ آ And Johnny like a senseless fool
آ آ آ آ Kept harping on the ghost.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Sheï؟½ll cross the moonlit road in haste
آ آ آ آ And vanish down the track;
آ آ آ آ Her long black hair hangs to her waist
آ آ آ آ And she is dressed in black;
آ آ آ آ Her face is white, a dull dead white,
آ آ آ آ Her eyes are opened wide,
آ آ آ آ She never looks to left or right,
آ آ آ آ Or turns to either side.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ I didnï؟½t bï؟½lieve in ghosts at all,
آ آ آ آ Thoï؟½ I was rather young,
آ آ آ آ But still I wished with all my heart
آ آ آ آ That Jack would hold his tongue.
آ آ آ آ The time and place, as you will say,
آ آ آ آ (ï؟½Twas twelve oï؟½clock almost),
آ آ آ آ Were both historically fa-
آ آ آ آ Vourable for a ghost.

آ آ آ آ But have you seen the Second Bridge
آ آ آ آ Beneath the ï؟½Camelï؟½s Backï؟½?
آ آ آ آ It fills a gap that broke the ridge
آ آ آ آ When convicts made the track;
آ آ آ آ And oï؟½er the right old Hartley Vale
آ آ آ آ In homely beauty lies,
آ آ آ آ And oï؟½er the left the mighty walls
آ آ آ آ Of Mount Victoria rise.

آ آ آ آ And thereï؟½s a spot above the bridge,
آ آ آ آ Just where the track is steep,
آ آ آ آ From which poor Convict Govett rode
آ آ آ آ To christen Govettï؟½s Leap;
آ آ آ آ And here a teamster killed his wife,
آ آ آ آ For those old days were rough,
آ آ آ آ And here a dozen others had
آ آ آ آ Been murdered, right enough.

آ آ آ آ The lonely moon was over all
آ آ آ آ And she was shining well,
آ آ آ آ At angles from the sandstone wall
آ آ آ آ The shifting moonbeams fell.
آ آ آ آ In short, the shifting moonbeams beamed,
آ آ آ آ The air was still as death,
آ آ آ آ Save when the listening silence seemed
آ آ آ آ To speak beneath its breath.

آ آ آ آ The tangled bushes were not stirred
آ آ آ آ Because there was no wind,
آ آ آ آ But now and then I thought I heard
آ آ آ آ A startling noise behind.
آ آ آ آ Then Johnny Jones began to quake;
آ آ آ آ His face was like the dead.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Donï؟½t look behind, for heavenï؟½s sake!
آ آ آ آ The ghost is there!ï؟½ he said.

آ آ آ آ He stared ahead, his eyes were fixed;
آ آ آ آ He whipped the horse like mad.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½You fool!ï؟½ I cried, ï؟½youï؟½re only mixed;
آ آ آ آ A drop too much youï؟½ve had.
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ll never see a ghost, I swear,
آ آ آ آ But I will find the cause.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ I turned to see if it was there,
آ آ آ آ And sure enough it was!

آ آ آ آ Its look appeared to plead for aid
آ آ آ آ (As far as I could see),
آ آ آ آ Its hands were on the tailboard laid,
آ آ آ آ Its eyes were fixed on me.
آ آ آ آ The face, it cannot be denied
آ آ آ آ Was white, a dull dead white,
آ آ آ آ The great black eyes were opened wide
آ آ آ آ And glistened in the light.

آ آ آ آ I stared at Jack; he stared ahead
آ آ آ آ And madly plied the lash.
آ آ آ آ To show I wasnï؟½t scared, I said,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Why, Jack, weï؟½ve made a mash.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ I tried to laugh; ï؟½twas vain to try.
آ آ آ آ The try was very lame;
آ آ آ آ And, thoï؟½ I wouldnï؟½t show it, I
آ آ آ آ Was frightened, all the same.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Sheï؟½s mashed,ï؟½ said Jack, ï؟½I do not doubt,
آ آ آ آ But ï؟½tis a lonely place;
آ آ آ آ And then you see it might turn out
آ آ آ آ A breach of promise case.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ He flogged the horse until it jibbed
آ آ آ آ And stood as one resigned,
آ آ آ آ And then he struck the road and ran
آ آ آ آ And left the cart behind.

آ آ آ آ Now, Jack and I since infancy
آ آ آ آ Had shared our joys and cares,
آ آ آ آ And so I was resolved that we
آ آ آ آ Should share each otherï؟½s scares.
آ آ آ آ We raced each other all the way
آ آ آ آ And never slept that night,
آ آ آ آ And when we told the tale next day
آ آ آ آ They said that we were, intoxicated.
meaning:
"The Ghost At The Second Bridge" is a quintessential example of Henry Lawson's signature style...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
